rationale: The citing paper references Fischer and Albers (2010) multiple times to define its conceptual boundary: it explicitly excludes category-level primary-demand and physician-oriented contexts and states that Fischer and Albers already covers that domain. It then positions its own contribution as complementary, shifting to brand-level selective-demand advertising elasticities across many consumer categories. The citation is also used as a quantitative benchmark when interpreting the observed median elasticity, indicating a meaningful interpretive role beyond a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing study substantively positions itself against Fischer and Albers (2010) as a complementary stream: Fischer and Albers is treated as the primary-demand pharmaceutical reference point, while the citing paper addresses selective-demand brand advertising across broader markets. It also uses Fischer and Albers' elasticity estimates for contextual comparison of effect sizes.
